body.raw:
{
  "appId": "69a04447c2f6d3e098135b35",
  "buttonId": "69a16102cef5996af9807c67",
  "title": "GURU NANAK DEV JI",
  "shortDescription": "Guru Nanak sits at the center with a parrot, flanked by Mardana with his sitar and Bala holding a peacock whisk.",
  "longDescription": "Guru Nanak (first Guru, 1469-1539) is depicted characteristically, seated in the middle with a parrot, attended by his musician Mardana, holding a sitar on the left, and devotee Bala on the right, holding a peacock whisk (chaur). Painted on rice paper and mounted on teak wood, this piece is extensively embellished with gold structures and embedded with semi-precious stones. Made for the Maharaja of Mysore, who had a collection of similar paintings of deities of India, this rare piece is created in the Thanjavur (Tanjore) style. The group is seated on a terrace underneath a gold canopy with winged angels flying above, representing divine authority. Exhibited at the Smithsonian ( National Museum of Natural History) in 'Sikhs : Legacy of the Punjab' July 25, 2004 - September 18,2007.",
  "image": "https://rosetta-stone-experience.s3.ap-southeast-2.amazonaws.com/materials/images/thumbnail_image/2fce90d7-a28a-4b10-956e-94d71a031b52.jpeg",
  "thumbnail": "https://rosetta-stone-experience.s3.ap-southeast-2.amazonaws.com/materials/images/thumbnail_image/2fce90d7-a28a-4b10-956e-94d71a031b52.jpeg",
  "accession": "MFGN-000",
  "period": "Circa 1800 / Punjab Plains, India",
  "credit": "Mahal Art Collection",
  "moreDetail": "www.sikhhistorymuseum.org"
}

response.name: Add the Gallery Content

body.raw:
{
  "appId": "69a04447c2f6d3e098135b35",
  "buttonId": "69a16102cef5996af9807c64",
  "title": "Gallery Title",
  "shortDescription": "Guru Angad, born Lehna in Punjab’s Malwa region, became a devoted follower of Guru Nanak after a life-changing meeting in Kartarpur. ",
  "longDescription": "A meditative Guru Nanak (first Guru, 1469-1539), seated on a platform is starring off into the distance with a focused glare, adorned in a white tunic (Jämä) and brown conical cap (seli topi). The Guru is seated on a reddish platform outlined in gold embroidery (zaradär) is resting upon a cushionwith another cushion under his arms for meditative support. A standing devotee on the right (BhaiBala) waves a whisk (chaur) over the Guru's halo, while a musician (Bhai mardana) dressed in green plays a rabäb, a lute. The group is covered by a canopy, symbolic of royalty, on a dark blue terrace,which provides a depth of composition in contrast to the flat reddish-orange background with faint clouds stretching across the sky.",
  "image": "https://...",
  "thumbnail": "https://...",
  "accession": "MFGN-013",
  "period": "Circa 1830 / Punjab Plains, India",
  "credit": "Mahal Art Collection",
  "moreDetail": "www.sikhhistorymuseum.org"
}
